subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference
- **Standard game** — 4 to 10 players.
- **Recommended** — 7 players.

#### Core Components
- **Red apple cards** — Hundreds of noun cards (people, places, things, events). These are the answer cards.
- **Green apple cards** — Hundreds of adjective cards (descriptive words like "scary," "delicious," "absurd"). These are the prompt cards.
- **Card trays** — Trays to hold the red and green card decks.
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

#### Card Details
- **Red cards** — Each shows a noun or noun phrase with a humorous or factual description.
- **Green cards** — Each shows an adjective that players will try to match.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setup Sequence
1. **Shuffle red cards** — Shuffle the red apple deck thoroughly and place in the tray.
2. **Shuffle green cards** — Shuffle the green apple deck and place in a separate tray.
3. **Deal red cards** — Deal 7 red apple cards to each player. Players may look at their own cards.
4. **Select first judge** — Choose the first judge by any method (e.g., most recent apple eater).

#### Table Layout
- **Red deck** — Central, within reach for drawing replacements.
- **Green deck** — Near the judge for flipping prompts.
- **Submission area** — A clear space where players place their red card submissions face-down.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Roles
- **Judge** — 1 player per round; rotates each round.
- **Players** — All other players submit red cards.

#### Hand Management
- **Starting hand** — 7 red apple cards per player.
- **Replenishment** — Draw back up to 7 after each round.
- **Judge's hand** — The judge keeps their hand but does not play a red card during their judging turn.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Pre-First-Turn Checks
- **All players have 7 red cards** — Verify hand size.
- **Judge identified** — First judge is selected.
- **Decks accessible** — Both trays are placed centrally.

#### First Action
1. **Judge flips green card** — The judge draws the top green apple card and reads the adjective aloud.
2. **Players select** — All other players choose one red card from their hand that they think best matches (or will most amuse the judge).

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Flow
1. **Flip green card** — Judge draws and reads aloud a green apple card (adjective).
2. **Submit red cards** — All other players choose one red card from their hand and place it face-down in the center.
3. **Shuffle submissions** — Judge collects all submitted red cards and shuffles them (to anonymize).
4. **Read aloud** — Judge reads each submitted red card aloud, often with commentary.
5. **Judge picks winner** — Judge selects the red card they think best matches the green card (funniest, most fitting, etc.).
6. **Award green card** — The player who submitted the winning red card takes the green card as a point.
7. **Draw back up** — All players who submitted draw one red card to return to 7.
8. **Rotate judge** — The player to the left of the current judge becomes the new judge.
9. **Discard red cards** — All non-winning submitted red cards go to the discard pile.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Player Actions
- **Select a card** — Choose one red card from your hand to submit.
- **Play to the judge** — Consider the judge's personality, humor, and preferences when choosing.
- **No table talk rules** — Players may lobby, argue, or make their case before the judge decides (house rule variant).

#### Judge Actions
- **Read all submissions** — Must read every submitted red card before deciding.
- **Pick favorite** — Choose based on any criteria: funniest, most accurate, most creative, etc.
- **Judge is final** — The judge's decision cannot be overruled.

#### Card Management
- **Hand limit** — Always 7 red cards in hand (replenish after submitting).
- **No trading** — Players may not trade or show red cards to each other.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Winning Threshold (by Player Count)
- **4 players** — First to 8 green cards wins.
- **5 players** — First to 7 green cards wins.
- **6 players** — First to 6 green cards wins.
- **7 players** — First to 5 green cards wins.
- **8+ players** — First to 4 green cards wins.

#### Judge Psychology
- **Know your judge** — The core meta-game is predicting what the current judge will find appealing.
- **Literal vs. funny** — Some judges pick the most accurate match; others pick the funniest or most absurd.
- **Lobbying** — Some groups allow players to argue for their submission after cards are read (before the judge decides).

#### Variant Rules
- **Apple Turnovers** — Play a red card face-down from the top of the deck as a wild/random entry.
- **Crab Apples** — Judge picks the LEAST matching card instead of the best.
- **2 for 1** — Players submit 2 red cards; judge picks a winning pair.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- **Race to threshold** — Game ends immediately when a player collects the required number of green cards.
- **Threshold varies** — Based on player count (see Special Mechanics).

#### Scoring Model
- **Green cards = points** — Each green card won equals 1 point.
- **First to threshold wins** — No further scoring needed.

#### Tiebreaker
- **Simultaneous win** — If two players reach the threshold in the same round, the judge picks between them in a head-to-head submission.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Early Game
- **Read the judge** — Pay attention to what kind of matches the judge prefers (literal, humorous, absurd).
- **Save strong cards** — If you have a universally funny red card, save it for when you know the judge will appreciate it.
- **Play weak cards on tough judges** — Burn bad red cards when the judge's taste is unpredictable.

#### Hand Management
- **Cycle your hand** — Playing even mediocre cards is better than hoarding, since you draw replacements.
- **Don't get attached** — Every red card you don't play is a missed opportunity to win a green card.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Playing to yourself** — Submitting cards YOU find funny instead of what the JUDGE will pick.
- **Ignoring judge rotation** — Not adapting your strategy when a new judge takes over.
- **Always playing literally** — The most accurate match rarely wins against a funnier, more creative submission.
- **Hoarding good cards** — Saving a perfect card for too long; the ideal green card may never come.
- **Not lobbying** — In groups that allow table talk, staying silent while others argue for their card is a disadvantage.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Funny vs. Accurate
- **Funny** — Go for the laugh if the judge values humor. Higher variance, higher reward.
- **Accurate** — Play the objectively best match if the judge is literal-minded. Consistent but less exciting.

#### Decision 2: Save or Spend
- **Save** — Hold a great red card for when you are confident it will win.
- **Spend** — Play it now because a matching green card may never appear.

#### Decision 3: As Judge
- **Reward creativity** — Picking creative answers encourages more interesting play from the group.
- **Reward accuracy** — Picking literal matches keeps the game grounded and fair-feeling.
